text: Welcome to Russismar Architecture. Today my guest is Nikolas Bill from Maxinear.
Welcome Nikolas. Thanks for having me. Nikolas, what are you doing at Maxinear?
Maxinear is a digital company of the CarMax Group and CarMax is one of the world leading
manufacturers of high-strength fasteners and other code-forming products. Okay,
cool. So is that one of the products? Yeah, that's one of the products that I've
brought for you. Essentially we are holding the world together, especially in
the automotive space, so if you have ever driven in a car it's very likely that
you have already used one of our products. Okay, cool. So tell me a little bit about
your architecture. What problem are you going to tackle with that? What I've
brought you here is a project that we call piece counting because we wanted to
count the products as they come out of our production machines in real time and
make this data available to our users, our operators in the factory, as well as
sending this data off to the ERP system once a charge or a container has been done.
Okay, got it. Cool. So let's walk us through the architecture. What's happening exactly?
Yeah, so we start off with our products like one of these bolts and what we did, we
attached sensors to our production machines and as CarMax has a rich long history, the
company is almost a century old, we have accumulated quite some different machines
over time. So it's not like we have one solution that can fit everywhere, but we
tried and tried out a few different sensor technologies to find the right way to
detect this. Okay, so what happens now when a bolt hits the sensor? What we do, we
have a light grid. This is essentially a metal frame and there are a lot of laser
beams next to it inside this metal frame and if one of these bolts falls through
this light grid, we get a pulse from this light grid. And this data is then collected
using a Greengrass function running on one of our edge gateways and we have one of
these edge gateways per machine. Okay, so now how do you get that information into the
cloud? What we have here is a Greengrass function and this Greengrass function
acts as our local counting engine. This transforms the data that can be changed from
changing from different sensor variants that we have into one type of message
that contains things like how many pieces have been produced over the last time slot,
what is the state of the machine overall and when has this happened. And then we
send this off into AWS using IoT Core. And in IoT Core we have a few rules that
essentially trigger a Lambda function and this Lambda function then derives from the current state and the changes that have been sent using these messages.
The new state of the machine. Okay, nice. So you now have a user in the factory, so how does he get that information then?

The user has an application using AWS Amplify and this is a smartphone application that he can use to see the current state and in almost real time and we have something of a delay like five to ten seconds for that.
Okay. And this is something that runs on every smartphone or tablet that is available on the shop floor.

This application then uses an API that we've provided to gather this data and behind the API gateway we have a few Lambda functions that then make this data that is in the DynamoDB available for the application for the user.
Okay, got it. So the final part was how do you get that information then into the ERP system?

The application itself doesn't only have a display functionality, it also has the possibility to report, for example, a container that has been filled in the process and it is then sent to the ERP system using another part of this API,
which translated into an SQS queue, which is later sent to the ERP system.
Okay, cool. Yeah, very interesting. So tell me, from idea to production, how much time did you actually need it?
I think we need to split that into parts. The first was the research, what kind of census do we have.
This took something like nine months because we had to try out different technologies like image recognition or other parts that did not work for us.
Once we had this, had an idea how to build this, this was a project of something like five months from the start until we had something running in the real world production,
which is really lightning fast for the manufacturing industry.
Okay, okay, amazing, great. So what kind of benefits did you see when you implemented that solution?
Of course, the first thing is for the operators and users who can now report something that they have produced in almost real time to the ERP system,
instead of having to go over a very long manual process. And the other thing that we have is now we can really have points to hook into to provide other use cases,
like predictions, how long it is going to take to finish the current production order or what to do next.
Once these parts have been produced, that's usually not the end of the production chain.
Okay, so you already have some more ideas what you can do in the future. So actually, what's next on your roadmap?
Of course, we're rolling this currently out into the whole CarMax organization worldwide. And then we want to go further into this predictive topic,

how to predict when something is ready for the next part. And we're currently building a smart factory platform soon. This is something where we can react to and for example call a forklift once a certain container has been filled to take it to the next production step.
Cool. Okay, Nicolas, thanks for your insights and thanks for being my guest.
You're welcome.
And thanks for watching This is my Architecture.
